learningGoal: How to crack any code by mapping each letter to its number
Why writing out the full table is your secret weapon against mistakes

screens.title: Can you crack the secret code?
screens.body:
Daisy is building her first mapping table.

Here are two coded (changed using a rule) words:

**SPOT = 1234**
**TOPS = 4321**

Each letter maps to exactly one number. Can you figure out what **POST** would be? Let's find out!

screens.title: Write out every pair
screens.body:
Let's crack **SPOT = 1234** and **TOPS = 4321**.

Here's the golden rule: write out **every** letter-number pair in a table. Trying to hold it in your head is where mistakes creep in!

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect! Line up, check, then decode — that's the recipe. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — always line up first, then check, then decode!

steps.text: Line up SPOT with 1234
Check with TOPS = 4321
steps.why: Every letter should match — if not, re-check!

steps.text: Now decode: POST = 2314
steps.why: Replace each letter using your table

screens.title: Your turn — build the table!
screens.body:
**SPOT = 1234**
**TOPS = 4321**

What is the code for **POTS**?

interaction.question: What is the code for POTS?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Using the same table: S=1, P=2, O=3, T=4. POTS = P(2) O(3) T(4) S(1) = 2341. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is 2341. Using the same table: S=1, P=2, O=3, T=4. POTS = P(2) O(3) T(4) S(1) = 2341. ✓

screens.title: You've got the recipe!
screens.body: Every time you see word-code pairs, follow these three steps and you'll nail it:

steps.text: 1. Write each letter underneath its number
steps.why: Line them up carefully — position matters!

steps.text: 2. Check your table against the second word
steps.why: If anything doesn't match, fix it before moving on

steps.text: 3. Replace each letter in the target word
steps.why: One letter at a time, using your table ✓

learningGoal: Why rushing is the number one reason people lose marks on these questions
How checking each letter one by one keeps you safe from silly mistakes

screens.title: Spot the mistake in "1234"!
screens.body:
Evie is checking her friend's code work.

The word-code pairs are:
**MADE = 1234**
**DAME = 3214**

The answer given for **MEAD** was **1234**. But that's wrong! Can you spot why?

screens.title: Where did it go wrong?
screens.body:
That's just MADE again! The friend mixed up the letter order — MEAD has a different order from MADE

The fix: **go letter by letter** through your table. Never guess or rush!

steps.text: Mapping: M=1, A=2, D=3, E=4
steps.why: Build the table first

steps.text: Wrong: MEAD = 1234
steps.why: That's just MADE again! The friend mixed up the letter order — MEAD has a different order from MADE

steps.text: Right: MEAD = 1423 ✓
steps.why: Go letter by letter through the word

screens.title: What's the correct code?
screens.body:
**MADE = 1234**
**DAME = 3214**

What is the **correct** code for **EDAM**?

interaction.question: What is the correct code for EDAM?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is 4321. M=1, A=2, D=3, E=4. EDAM = E(4) D(3) A(2) M(1) = 4321. ✓

screens.title: Stay sharp — avoid these traps!
screens.body: Now you know the common mistakes, you can dodge them every time:

steps.text: 1. Go letter by letter — don't rush
steps.why: MEAD ≠ MADE — the order matters!

steps.text: 2. Check your table against BOTH words
steps.why: If it doesn't match, fix it before decoding

steps.text: 3. Spell the target word carefully
steps.why: Write it out, then replace each letter one at a time ✓

learningGoal: How repeated letters give you a brilliant shortcut to crack codes faster
How to spot 'anchor' letters that unlock the whole puzzle in seconds

screens.title: Spot the sneaky repeated letter!
screens.body:
Daisy is spotting repeated letters to crack codes faster.

**MOON = 1223**
**NOON = 3223**

**Did you know?** When a letter appears twice in a word, its matching number also appears twice in the code. That's your shortcut! Can you spot that **O** appears **twice**?

screens.title: Repeated letters = instant anchors
screens.body:
Look at **MOON** — the letter **O** appears **twice**, and so does the number **2** in its code.

When a letter appears twice in a word, its number **must also appear twice** in the same positions. This gives you an instant anchor to start building your table!

interaction.sentence: A repeated letter gives you an instant ____ to start building your mapping table
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! A repeated letter is an anchor — it locks in one mapping immediately. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — a repeated letter is called an anchor because it fixes one mapping straight away!

steps.text: "O" appears twice → find 2 appearing twice
steps.why: O=2 — that's your anchor!

steps.text: Fill in the rest: M=1, O=2, N=3
steps.why: Now the other letters fall into place

steps.text: Decode: MONO = 1232
steps.why: Replace each letter using the table ✓

screens.title: Your turn — use the anchor!
screens.body:
**MOON = 1223**
**NOON = 3223**

Use the repeated **O** to crack the code. What is **MONO**?

interaction.question: What is the code for MONO?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! O appears twice in MOON — and 2 appears twice in 1223. So O=2. From position: M=1, N=3. MONO = M(1) O(2) N(3) O(2) = 1232. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is 1232. O appears twice in MOON — and 2 appears twice in 1223. So O=2. From position: M=1, N=3. MONO = M(1) O(2) N(3) O(2) = 1232. ✓

screens.title: Repeated letters — your superpower!
screens.body: Top coders always scan for repeated letters first. Once you spot one, the rest of the puzzle falls into place:

steps.text: 1. Scan both the word AND the code for repeats
steps.why: MOON has two O's; 1223 has two 2's

steps.text: 2. Match the repeated letter to the repeated number
steps.why: That's your anchor — the first pair you know for sure

steps.text: 3. Fill in the rest from there
steps.why: One anchor makes the whole table easier ✓

learningGoal: How to work backwards from a mystery number code to uncover the hidden word
How to flip your mapping table around so numbers point back to letters

screens.title: What word is hiding in 321?
screens.body:
Ella is decoding a mystery number back into a word.

**BAD = 123**

Here's a fun twist — this time you're going **backwards**! You know the code **321**, but what word is hiding inside it?

screens.title: Build the table, then read it backwards
screens.body:
We know **BAD = 123** — now we need to work out what word **321** stands for.

The method is the same — build your mapping table first. Then for each **number** in the code, find the matching **letter**.

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect! Build, flip, replace — that's how you reverse decode. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — build the table first, then flip it, then replace each digit!

steps.text: Build the table: B=1, A=2, D=3
steps.why: Same table-building step as always

steps.text: Now flip it: 1→B, 2→A, 3→D
steps.why: Number → letter instead of letter → number

steps.text: 321 = DAB
steps.why: Replace each digit with its letter ✓

screens.title: Your turn — decode the number!
screens.body:
**GOD = 437**

What word does **734** stand for?

interaction.question: What word does 734 stand for?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! From GOD=437: G=4, O=3, D=7. Reverse: 7=D, 3=O, 4=G. So 734 = DOG. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is DOG. From GOD=437: G=4, O=3, D=7. Reverse: 7=D, 3=O, 4=G. So 734 = DOG. ✓

screens.title: Reverse decode — you've got this!
screens.body: When the question gives you a number and asks for the word, just follow these steps:

steps.text: 1. Build the mapping table as normal
steps.why: Letter → number from the given word-code pair

steps.text: 2. Flip it: number → letter
steps.why: 1→C, 2→A, 3→R, 4→E (for example)

steps.text: 3. Replace each digit in the code with its letter
steps.why: Spell out the word one letter at a time ✓

learningGoal: How two words that share letters give you a powerful way to double-check your work
How shared letters between word pairs help you discover new mappings with confidence

screens.title: What do these words have in common?
screens.body:
Daisy is cross-referencing shared letters between two words.

**CART = 1234**
**CARD = 1235**

Spot it? These words share the letters **C, A, R**! When two words share letters, those letters have the **same numbers** in both codes. That's like having a built-in answer checker!

screens.title: Shared letters confirm your table
screens.body:
**CART** and **CARD** both contain the letters **C, A, R** — and those letters have the **same numbers** in both codes.

This lets you:
1. Confirm your mapping is right
2. Spot the **new** letter (**D**) that's different

steps.text: Shared: C, A, R — same numbers in both codes
steps.why: These are confirmed

steps.text: New letter: D=5
steps.why: The only letter that changed → the only number that changed

steps.text: Decode: DART = 5234
steps.why: Now you have all the letters you need ✓

screens.title: Your turn — cross-reference!
screens.body:
**CART = 1234**
**CARD = 1235**

What is the code for **DART**?

interaction.question: What is the code for DART?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! CART and CARD share C=1, A=2, R=3. CART has T=4; CARD has D=5 (the only different letter/number). DART = D(5) A(2) R(3) T(4) = 5234. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is 5234. CART and CARD share C=1, A=2, R=3. CART has T=4; CARD has D=5 (the only different letter/number). DART = D(5) A(2) R(3) T(4) = 5234. ✓

screens.title: Cross-referencing — a pro move!
screens.body: When two words share letters, you've got a really handy trick:

steps.text: 1. Spot the letters that appear in BOTH words
steps.why: CART and CARD share C, A, R

steps.text: 2. Check their numbers match in both codes
steps.why: Same letter = same number — if not, re-check!

steps.text: 3. The different letter gets the different number
steps.why: Only one letter changed, so only one number changed ✓

learningGoal: How to handle 4-letter word codes like a pro
How to use the table method to crack any 4-letter word code quickly

screens.title: Crack the 4-letter code!
screens.body:
Daisy is working with 4-letter word codes.

**TRAM = 8524**

Four letters, four pairs to track — but you've already done this with 3 letters, so you've got this! Can you decode **MART**?

screens.title: 4 letters, 4 pairs
screens.body: From **TRAM = 8524**, line up each letter with its number — there are exactly 4 pairs to write down.

steps.text: TRAM = 8524
Now decode MART letter by letter
MART = 4258 ✓
steps.why: Four pairs, four digits — done!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
**TRAM = 8524**

What is the code for **MART**?

interaction.question: What is the code for MART?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! From TRAM=8524: T=8, R=5, A=2, M=4. MART = M(4) A(2) R(5) T(8) = 4258. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is 4258. From TRAM=8524: T=8, R=5, A=2, M=4. MART = M(4) A(2) R(5) T(8) = 4258. ✓

screens.title: 4-letter codes — easy when you're organised!
screens.body: Keep it neat and you'll breeze through 4-letter codes:

steps.text: 1. Write the word out: T R A M
steps.why: Leave space between letters

steps.text: 2. Write the code below: 8 5 2 4
steps.why: Each number goes under its letter

steps.text: 3. Read off the target word using your pairs

learningGoal: How to handle the longest word codes without breaking a sweat
Why staying organised is your biggest advantage with longer words

screens.title: Ready for the 5-letter challenge?
screens.body:
Daisy is tackling 5-letter codes for the first time.

**PLATE = 65289**
**PETAL = 69825**

5 letters means 5 pairs — but the method is exactly the same! If you can do 4, you can definitely do 5. Let's go!

screens.title: 5 letters — stay organised!
screens.body: From **PLATE = 65289**, we need 5 pairs to decode **LEAPT**. With 5 letters, it's even more important to write out your full table. Don't skip any pairs!

steps.text: Map all 5: P=6, L=5, A=2, T=8, E=9
steps.why: Write every single pair

steps.text: Check with second word: PETAL=69825
steps.why: All 5 should match — if not, fix your table

steps.text: Decode: LEAPT = 59268
steps.why: Replace letter by letter, left to right ✓

screens.title: Your turn — 5-letter challenge!
screens.body:
**PLATE = 65289**
**PETAL = 69825**

What is the code for **LEAPT**?

interaction.question: What is the code for LEAPT?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! From PLATE=65289: P=6, L=5, A=2, T=8, E=9. Check PETAL=69825: P(6) E(9) T(8) A(2) L(5) — confirmed! LEAPT = L(5) E(9) A(2) P(6) T(8) = 59268. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is 59268. From PLATE=65289: P=6, L=5, A=2, T=8, E=9. Check PETAL=69825: P(6) E(9) T(8) A(2) L(5) — confirmed! LEAPT = L(5) E(9) A(2) P(6) T(8) = 59268. ✓

screens.title: 5-letter codes — you've conquered them!
screens.body: These are the toughest codes you'll see, and you now know exactly how to beat them:

steps.text: 1. Write ALL 5 pairs in your table
steps.why: Don't try to remember them — write them down!

steps.text: 2. Use the second word to double-check
steps.why: If one pair doesn't match, fix it immediately

steps.text: 3. Decode left to right, one letter at a time
steps.why: No shortcuts — patience wins ✓

subConcepts.name: Anagram Codes — Same Letters, Different Order

learningGoal: How anagram words (same letters, different order) always use the same digits reshuffled
Why spotting anagrams is one of the fastest shortcuts in code-cracking

screens.title: Same letters, same digits — cool, right?
screens.body:
Daisy is spotting anagram patterns in codes.

**STOP = 4826**
**TOPS = 8264**

**Did you know?** These words are **anagrams** — they use the **same letters** shuffled into a different order. And here's the brilliant part: their codes use the **same digits** shuffled too!

screens.title: Anagram shortcut
screens.body: **STOP** and **TOPS** are anagrams (words made from the same letters in a different order) — and their codes use the **same digits rearranged**. This means you only need ONE mapping table for ALL the anagrams!

steps.text: Build the table from STOP: S=4, T=8, O=2, P=6
steps.why: One table works for all the anagrams

steps.text: TOPS uses the same digits: 8264
steps.why: Same letters → same digits, just shuffled

steps.text: POST = 6248
steps.why: Apply the same table to any anagram ✓

screens.title: Your turn — decode the anagram!
screens.body:
**STOP = 4826**
**TOPS = 8264**

What is the code for **POST**?

(Hint: it uses the same digits as the other words!)

interaction.question: What is the code for POST?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! STOP, TOPS, POTS, POST — all anagrams using S=4, T=8, O=2, P=6. They all use the same digits (2,4,6,8) in different orders. POST = P(6) O(2) S(4) T(8) = 6248. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is 6248. STOP, TOPS, POTS, POST — all anagrams using S=4, T=8, O=2, P=6. They all use the same digits (2,4,6,8) in different orders. POST = P(6) O(2) S(4) T(8) = 6248. ✓

screens.title: Anagram codes — a brilliant shortcut!
screens.body: Remember this trick and you'll save loads of time in the exam:

steps.text: Same letters → same digits
steps.why: STOP/TOPS/POTS/POST all use 2, 4, 6, 8

steps.text: Different order → different arrangement
steps.why: The digits rearrange just like the letters do

steps.text: One table decodes ALL the anagrams
steps.why: Build it once, use it for every version ✓

learningGoal: How to use elimination to find the right answer when all the options look similar
How to knock out wrong answers one by one like a detective

screens.title: Too many options? Time to eliminate!
screens.body:
Daisy is using elimination to find the right answer.

**PART = 6238**

What is the code for **TRAP**?

Options: **8326**  |  **8362**  |  **8236**  |  **8632**  |  **3826**

They all look really similar! But don't panic — there's a smart way to do this. Instead of guessing, **cross out** the wrong ones step by step.

screens.title: Check one position at a time
screens.body:
From **PART = 6238**, we need to find **TRAP**.

Options: **8326**  |  **8362**  |  **8236**  |  **8632**  |  **3826**

Look at each position. If an option has the **wrong digit** in ANY position, cross it out!

steps.text: T=8, so the code MUST start with 8 → eliminates 3826
steps.why: Cross it out!

steps.text: R=3, so the second digit MUST be 3 → eliminates 8236 and 8632
steps.why: Cross it out!

steps.text: A=2, P=6, so it ends in 26 → eliminates 8362
steps.why: Cross it out!

steps.text: TRAP = 8326 ✓
steps.why: Last one standing!

screens.title: Your turn — eliminate!
screens.body:
**PART = 6238**

What is the correct answer for **TRAP**?

Use elimination — check each position!

interaction.question: What is the correct code/word for TRAP?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! P=6, A=2, R=3, T=8. TRAP starts with T→8, then R→3, then A→2, then P→6. So TRAP = 8326. Check each position to eliminate wrong answers! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is 8326. P=6, A=2, R=3, T=8. TRAP starts with T→8, then R→3, then A→2, then P→6. So TRAP = 8326. Check each position to eliminate wrong answers! ✓

screens.title: Elimination — your safety net!
screens.body: When answers look similar, elimination has your back:

steps.text: 1. Check the FIRST digit/letter
steps.why: This alone often eliminates 2-3 options

steps.text: 2. Check the SECOND digit/letter
steps.why: Usually narrows it to 1-2 options

steps.text: 3. Verify the full code matches
steps.why: The last one standing is your answer ✓

learningGoal: How to stay cool and use elimination even on the trickiest code questions
Why you should never panic when answers look almost identical

screens.title: They all look so similar... but you can do this!
screens.body:
Evie is facing five very similar-looking options.

**GUST = 8694**

What is the code for **GUTS**?

Options: **8649**  |  **8694**  |  **8469**  |  **8496**  |  **8946**

They look nearly identical, but stay calm! Check **one position at a time** and the wrong ones will fall away.

screens.title: Position by position
screens.body:
From **GUST = 8694**, we need to decode **GUTS**.

Options: **8649**  |  **8694**  |  **8469**  |  **8496**  |  **8946**

When options look nearly identical, go through **position by position**. Each check eliminates more wrong answers!

steps.text: All start with 8 (G=8) — so check the second digit
steps.why: Eliminate!

steps.text: U=6, so second digit is 6 → eliminates 8469, 8496, 8946
steps.why: Eliminate!

steps.text: T=4, S=9: ends in 49 → eliminates 8694 (that's GUST again!)
steps.why: Eliminate!

steps.text: GUTS = 8649 ✓
steps.why: Found it!

screens.title: Your turn — eliminate step by step!
screens.body:
**GUST = 8694**

What is the code for **GUTS**?

interaction.question: What is the code for GUTS?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! G=8, U=6, S=9, T=4. GUTS = G(8) U(6) T(4) S(9) = 8649. Watch out — 8694 is GUST, not GUTS! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is 8649. G=8, U=6, S=9, T=4. GUTS = G(8) U(6) T(4) S(9) = 8649. Watch out — 8694 is GUST, not GUTS! ✓

screens.title: Smart coders don't guess — they eliminate!
screens.body: When options look nearly the same, here's your winning strategy:

steps.text: 1. Check position 1 across all options
steps.why: Usually the same — move to position 2

steps.text: 2. Check position 2 — this often eliminates half
steps.why: One wrong digit = one wrong answer

steps.text: 3. Keep going until only ONE option fits
steps.why: Systematic elimination beats guessing every time ✓
